full_response:
Assumptions  
1. Work in an inertial “lab” frame S (the ground).  
2. Knight #2 rides in the +x direction with speed v relative to S.  
3. Knight #1 rides in the −x direction with the same speed v relative to S, so the two knights are heading directly toward one another.

Goal  
Find the speed u with which Knight #1 approaches Knight #2 as measured in Knight #2’s rest frame S′.

Relativistic velocity‐addition formula (one–dimensional)  
If an object moves with speed u along x in the lab and the primed frame moves with speed v along +x with respect to the lab, then the object’s speed in the primed frame is  

u′ = (u − v)/(1 − uv/c²).

Application to the knights  
• Lab speed of Knight #1: u = −v (negative sign because he moves toward −x).  
• Lab speed of Knight #2 (speed of S′ relative to S): v.

Plug in:

u′ = (−v − v) / [1 − (−v)(v)/c²]
    = (−2v) / [1 + v²/c²].

The minus sign merely tells us Knight #1 is approaching Knight #2 (coming from the −x direction).  
The magnitude of the relative speed is therefore

u = |u′| = 2v / (1 + v²/c²).

Special cases  
• v ≪ c  ⇒  u ≈ 2v  (classical result).  
• v → c  ⇒  u → c  (never exceeds the speed of light).

\boxed{\,u = \dfrac{2v}{1 + v^{2}/c^{2}}\,}

Confidence: 70%
